title: Distractor Documents
concept: How irrelevant but topically similar text in context measurably lowers answer accuracy.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: in the 'lost-in-the-middle' effect, which part of the context window tends to get the LEAST attention from the model?
exercises.explain: Models tend to attend most strongly to the start and end of the context, so information buried in the middle is easiest to miss or 'forget.'

cards.title: Same Topic, Wrong Answer
cards.body: You ask an AI about your company's refund policy and give it 5 documents. One has the real answer. The other 4 are ALSO about refunds — just not the one you need. The AI mixes them up and answers wrong.

cards.title: What's a Distractor Document?
cards.body: A distractor document is a piece of context that's topically similar to the relevant one but doesn't actually contain the answer. It's not irrelevant garbage — it's relevant-LOOKING text that competes for the model's attention and drags accuracy down.

cards.title: The Lookalike Suspects
cards.body: Imagine a detective lineup where five suspects look almost identical to the real culprit. Even a great detective slows down and second-guesses themselves. Distractor documents are the lookalike suspects of your context window — the model has to work harder to pick the right one, and sometimes it picks wrong.

cards.title: Real Example
cards.body: Ask 'What's the refund window for electronics?' Give the model your electronics policy plus policies for clothing, furniture, and appliances returns. Even though only one document has the real answer, the model sometimes blends numbers from the similar-sounding policies and gives a wrong window.

cards.title: Myth: More Relevant Context = Better Answers
cards.body: You'd think giving the model MORE documents about the topic would help it answer better. Actually, studies show accuracy drops as you add more topically-similar-but-irrelevant documents — even if the correct one stays in the mix. Quantity of 'related' text isn't the same as quality of relevant text.

cards.title: Clean Context vs. Distractor-Filled Context
cards.body: Same question, same correct document — but very different accuracy depending on what else is in the context.

left.text: Model answers correctly ~95% of the time — nothing competes for attention.

right.text: Accuracy drops sharply — the model confuses details across similar-sounding documents.

exercises.prompt: Why are distractor documents especially tricky for an LLM compared to totally random, unrelated text?

options.text: They use more tokens than random text
They're topically similar, so the model can't easily tell them apart from the real answer
They are always placed in the middle of the context
They are written in a different language

exercises.explain: Distractors are dangerous precisely because they LOOK relevant — same topic, similar wording — so the model has to work harder to distinguish them from the document that actually holds the answer, and it sometimes fails.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Adding more documents about the same general topic always improves an LLM's answer accuracy, as long as the correct document is included somewhere.
exercises.explain: False. Research shows that adding more topically-similar distractor documents tends to LOWER accuracy, even when the correct document is present, because the model has more similar text to sift through and confuse.

exercises.sentence: A distractor document is dangerous because it is topically similar to the real answer, causing the model to confuse it with the correct document.
exercises.explain: The defining trait of a distractor is that it's topically similar to the correct document — not random noise — which is exactly why it's so easy for the model to mix up.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three test setups is most likely AI-generated to specifically probe for the distractor-document effect?

options.text: A single question with one clearly correct document and no other context provided.
A benchmark with one correct document plus several near-duplicate documents on the same topic but with subtly different facts, testing if the model picks the right one.
A casual chat log between two friends discussing their weekend plans.

exercises.explain: Option B is a deliberately engineered test: near-duplicate, same-topic documents with subtly different facts are a classic way researchers construct distractor-document benchmarks to measure accuracy degradation.

exercises.prompt: You're building a support bot. It retrieves 8 documents for every question: 1 has the exact answer, 7 are about similar-but-different topics (e.g., other product lines' policies). Accuracy is poor. Based on what you've learned about lost-in-the-middle AND distractor documents, what's the BEST fix?

options.text: Add even more similar documents so the model has 'more context' to work with
Improve retrieval to fetch fewer, more precisely relevant documents, and place the best one near the start or end of the context
Translate all documents into a different language before feeding them in
Increase the model's temperature setting so it explores more possibilities

exercises.explain: Two effects are stacking against you: distractor documents make it hard for the model to pick the right facts among similar-looking ones, and lost-in-the-middle means position matters too. The fix addresses both — tighten retrieval so fewer, more precise documents are passed in (less competition from distractors), and position the most relevant one at the start or end where attention is strongest. Piling on more similar text or fiddling with temperature won't fix a context-quality problem.
